svg.text: A first-pool spread model predicts where an unseen condition lands; separate noise restores how the path wanders
Fit on other runs, the simulator observes only the held-out condition's FIRST pool, holds spread fixed, and rolls the
selection/update equations forward — reading none of that run's later candidates, spread, agreement, or value. Validation
is leave-one-condition-out: the entire intervention / judge regime is held out. Endpoint MAE = mean absolute error of the
predicted final behavioural value vs the true final value, on the 0-1 scale; lower is better.
Does the closed loop predict an unseen condition from its first pool?
Endpoint MAE per regime: the frozen mean within-prompt population-SD model (rolled forward from round 1) vs a no-change
baseline that predicts the round-1 value forever. It wins in every selection and intervention regime, and ties the weak
first-pool spread model — rolls forward from round 1
no-change baseline — predicts the round-1 value forever
endpoint MAE (predicted minus true final value, absolute) — lower is better
Out of scope from round 1: a mid-run judge change
When the judge is swapped mid-run, the round-1 rollout cannot see the new judge's scale, so it loses to no-change — the
only regime where the model is beaten.
Restart at the judge change repairs it
Remeasure the full state on the first pool the replacement judge scores, then roll forward from there.
endpoint MAE on the 9 judge-swap runs — restarting recovers 6/7 post-swap directions (movements ≥ 0.15) and observes one pool under the replacement judge.
What the rollout reproduces: coarse endpoints from the mean, realistic wandering from noise
On the 45 selection-driven + judge-swap runs, the deterministic conditional-mean path nails the coarse endpoint but is far
too smooth. A staged stochastic model — selector-gap and generator-mean residuals, a zero-mean agreement innovation around
persistence, and finite-battery observation noise — restores the measured path and calibrated endpoint intervals.
sum of absolute value-changes over the
number of times the trajectory reverses
share of runs inside the nominal-80%
The deterministic mean gets the coarse endpoint
36 of 38 large-movement directions correct; 19 of 24 observed rail
endpoints recovered. Predicted endpoint mean 0.572 vs observed
Selector-gap and generator-mean residuals, a zero-mean
agreement innovation around persistence, and observation
noise — battery readout only, it does not feed back. No
latent value-process kick and no risk-feedback term are
added: both are rejected as post-hoc.
Spread definition is a robustness check only: mean within-prompt population SD and mean within-prompt range give endpoint forecasts
differing by 0.0066 on average and share the same endpoint class on 66/67 runs, so the writeup uses mean SD for both the selector
